ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for slow-cooked beltfish (hairtail) with pancakes. FOODON_03302050 (braised fish food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for slow-braised beltfish with pancakes as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific composite dish.

serving.description: 1 shared portion (approximately 400 g including fish and pancakes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving is approximately 400 g, comprising slow-braised beltfish pieces with edible softened bones and two to three handmade wheat pancakes, as typically served as a shared dish for two people.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Hairtail fish (cooked) and wheat flatbread composite estimation; USDA SR Legacy item 15088 (Fish, tuna, light, canned in water) used as secondary anchor; primary estimation from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15230 (Fish, hairtail, cooked) and USDA SR Legacy flatbread values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked hairtail/beltfish (Trichiurus lepturus, approximately 60% of dish weight) and wheat-flour handmade pancakes (approximately 40% of dish weight), with adjustment for braising oil, soy sauce, and sugar. Beltfish nutrient reference: USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174204 (Fish, hairtail, raw) with cooking adjustment factors per USDA SR Legacy cooking yield tables. Pancake reference: USDA SR Legacy item 18364 (Bread, flatbread). Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat flatbread / mixed fish-grain dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for slow-cooked beltfish with handmade pancakes. GI estimated at 52 (low range) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being handmade wheat pancakes (analogous to chapati/flatbread, GI ~52 per University of Sydney GI Database) and the moderating effect of the high-protein braised fish component, fat content, and vinegar/soy sauce in the braising liquid, all of which are documented to lower postprandial glycemic response. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as (52 × 49.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 25.8, rounded to 26.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g per sitting, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1680 mg per 400 g serving) approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced soy sauce or a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from handmade wheat pancakes with low-GI moderating effect from fish protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The handmade wheat pancakes contribute a moderate carbohydrate load (approximately 49.6 g per 400 g serving) with an estimated GI of 52; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit pancake portions and pair with the fish component to leverage the protein- and fat-mediated blunting of postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from beltfish offset by high sodium and moderate saturated fat from braising oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beltfish provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from soy-based braising and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil represent cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in reduced portions, preferring the fish over the pancakes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Slow-Cooked Tender Beltfish with Handmade Pancakes (有局秘制酥闷带鱼配手工饼) is a traditional Beijing-style Chinese dish featuring beltfish (hairtail fish, Trichiurus lepturus) that is first lightly fried and then slow-braised in a seasoned soy sauce, cooking wine, ginger, garlic, and spice mixture until the fish bones become completely soft and edible. The extended slow-cooking process, a hallmark of old Beijing home cooking, transforms the typically sharp and inedible bones into a tender, calcium-rich, crunchy element that is consumed along with the flesh. The dish is served with handmade wheat flour pancakes that are used to wrap or accompany the fish, providing a textural contrast between the savoury, umami-rich braised fish and the soft, slightly chewy flatbread. The flavour profile is deeply savoury, mildly sweet from added sugar in the braising liquid, and richly aromatic from the spice blend. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate to high protein from the fish (approximately 13.5 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrates from the pancakes, and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids. The calcium content is notably elevated compared to most fish dishes due to the consumption of softened bones. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low range), moderated by the protein and fat content of the fish component. This dish is culturally significant as a representation of traditional Beijing slow-cooking techniques and is served in Singapore at the restaurant See Ya Tonight (今晚有局), which specialises in old Beijing-style cuisine.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked beltfish/hairtail and wheat flatbread components with braising adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate based on the wheat flatbread component moderated by fish protein and fat. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources (USDA FoodData Central) and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked hairtail/beltfish (approximately 60% dish weight) and wheat flatbread (approximately 40% dish weight), adjusted for braising oil, soy sauce, and sugar additions. No single approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as the dish uses vegetable cooking oil with negligible industrial trans fat content. 3. Calcium content (45 mg per 100 g) is elevated relative to typical fish dishes due to the consumption of slow-softened fish bones, which are a documented source of bioavailable calcium. 4. Sodium content is high (420 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce in the braising liquid; this is a primary clinical concern. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat flatbread (chapati analogue, GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the high-protein, high-fat fish component. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not individually confirmed from a single source entry; these values carry medium confidence. 7. The dish is sold at SGD 49.40 at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) in Singapore, consistent with a premium shared-portion restaurant dish. 8. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains soy sauce which may contain alcohol in some formulations, and the restaurant's halal certification status is not verified from approved sources.
